title: Wild-Time Temporal Distribution-Shift Benchmark

data_urls.access_notes: Project site links benchmark code and paper.
Use official loaders and task documentation.

instructions: Use public Wild-Time tasks first and inspect each task's upstream access requirements.
Record timestamp, domain/time split, task name, features, labels, and source dataset license.
Treat provided temporal splits as operating points and construct continuous time-distance/support curves when possible.

scale.download_size: variable by task; use one public task first

recommended_axes.name: temporal_distance
recommended_axes.definition: Distance in time between test samples and the nearest or distributional support of training samples.

recommended_axes.name: time_period_domain_support
recommended_axes.definition: Similarity of coarse eras, seasons, years, or temporal environments.

recommended_axes.name: feature_distribution_over_time
recommended_axes.definition: Train-fitted feature or embedding distribution support as time advances.

useful_for: Auditing deployment-time generalization across multiple modalities.
Testing whether temporal splits represent a continuous performance decay over time.
Dataset Scout fallback when a model claim is explicitly about robustness over time.
leakage_risks: Some tasks may include protected or credentialed upstream data.
Randomizing across time can leak future distribution information.
Temporal labels may be confounded with changes in class prevalence or annotation policy.

paperclip_provenance.notes: Paperclip identified Wild-Time as a temporal distribution-shift benchmark; official sources identify the project site and GitHub.
